review_text: Can’t trust anyone that says that this isn’t a great movie.

Yeah, this movie is great, definitely not as good as the previous two entries, though. Many movie trilogies always have an odd duck in the mix that brings down the whole franchise, but this, this is definitely not the case with Nolan’s Batman trilogy. I’d give this movie an 8.5 or 9 out of 10. Highly recommended.

The Dark Knight Rises, a great send off to the Nolan Batman trilogy. This film is fantastic. When I saw this first film for the first time. I shockingly enjoyed it. Because the ending of The Dark Knight made me be like there can’t be a third one. Christian Bale does amazing as Batman. The action scenes were great. Tom Hardy does a great Bane. And, that fight between Batman and Bane in the sewers was so rewatchable. Overall, I give this film a 9.3/10 or a 4.5/5 stars.

A satisfying conclusion to the trilogy, but easily the most flawed of the three. It’s kinda like Return of the Jedi, but even more so. A solid film, but one that had to cut a lot of corners that Nolan is known for not making in order to achieve its goals. But in the end, it’s still satisfying.

In spectacular fashion, Christopher Nolan produced not only a great film, but an equally great Batman story and satisfyingly epic conclusion to the trilogy. The movie experience, in my opinion, always enhances the the film, but this one holds special memories. The theater in my town ran a very cool special, screening Batman Begins and The Dark Knight directly before a midnight premier of Rises. As a huge fan, this was a special treat — free unlimited refills, hanging out with friends — the movie experience here produced a lasting memory with lifelong friends.

This movie is honestly a really risky finale.

A great ending to the trilogy

This is the final installment in Christopher Nolan’s Batman trilogy and I am a sucker for any Nolan film. Although this is not quite as good as The Dark Knight which would be nearly impossible to top it is still an exhilarating finish to the trilogy. The film builds a long mysterious plot around how Bruce Wayne’s story may come full circle and the journey to that ending is incredibly rewarding. The acting across the board is amazing. Christian Bale is the perfect Batman in my eyes capturing Bruce’s deepest priorities while maintaining the powerful mystique of the character. Tom Hardy plays the perfect Bane who is ruthless yet eerily calm a villain that feels truly unstoppable. The ending in typical Nolan fashion leaves the viewer’s imagination wondering for years after the film ends about what truly happened which makes it even more memorable. It is a powerful and satisfying conclusion to one of the greatest superhero trilogies ever made. PS I always loved how Batman somehow remained a mystery while flying around in the most advanced vehicles on Earth. Like who could it possibly be. Maybe the billionaire recluse with unlimited resources but that is why it is a movie. 90/100

Story: 9/10 – A satisfying conclusion to Nolan’s trilogy. Themes of redemption, sacrifice, and legacy are strong, though a few plot points feel rushed. Visuals: 9.5/10 – Epic city-wide destruction, practical stunts, and breathtaking IMAX shots. Gotham feels alive and huge. Soundtrack: 9.5/10 – Zimmer delivers another powerful score that builds tension and emotion beautifully. Action: 9/10 – Intense and cinematic, with memorable sequences like the plane and the sewers. Practical effects shine. Performances: 9/10 – Christian Bale closes his arc perfectly. Tom Hardy’s Bane is menacing, Anne Hathaway’s Catwoman is sleek and fun, and Michael Caine provides heart. Overall, 9/10
Everyone should watch this movie once in their life. Thanks Christopher Nolan ❤️

Critics Consensus: Ambitious, emotional, and visually grand, The Dark Knight Rises delivers a powerful and fitting conclusion to Christopher Nolan’s groundbreaking trilogy—though its sprawling scope occasionally strains under the weight of its own ambition. Set eight years after The Dark Knight, the film finds a weary, reclusive Bruce Wayne (Christian Bale) drawn back into battle as Gotham faces its greatest threat yet: Bane, a calculating brute brought chillingly to life by Tom Hardy. Bale once again brings depth and humanity to his portrayal, balancing vulnerability and strength as Batman grapples with legacy, pain, and redemption. Anne Hathaway’s sly and sharp Selina Kyle injects wit and intrigue, while Joseph Gordon-Levitt’s idealistic John Blake provides the film’s moral compass. Nolan crafts a city on the brink with his signature precision—combining practical effects, sweeping IMAX visuals, and a thunderous Hans Zimmer score to create an atmosphere of epic finality. Though the story occasionally buckles under exposition and a few narrative leaps, its emotional and thematic payoff is undeniable. The film closes the trilogy with resonance and catharsis, turning the tale of the Dark Knight into one of hope, endurance, and rebirth. Verdict: The Dark Knight Rises may not match the flawless intensity of its predecessor, but it soars on scale, emotion, and spectacle—a bold, heartfelt finale worthy of the legend.
Best movie in the franchise imho! Must see!!

Arguably just as good as The Dark Knight. Emotionally resonant, hopeful, and beautifully shot. It is almost 3 hours long but does not drag at all. Ends the trilogy perfectly. Those who say this isn't the best title in the Trilogy should probably use context. For one, Heath Ledger WAS going to be in this movie, in what capacity is unknown but I'd think pretty major. Second, this was going to be the final movie in the trilogy and how it ended was a superb close. Unlike The Dark Knight and more like Begins, this movie had the theme of overcoming Fear with the addition of overcoming self-righteousness as well. Finally, the character of Bane is absolutely a screen stealer as well, and does rival the screen presence of the Joker. While it's sad we'll never know what we could've gotten with Heath being here, the Rises movie as it stands is still a powerful movie and a close to one of the best trilogies ever made.
A flawed but emotionally resonant epic. While it lacks The Dark Knight’s tight scripting and occationally suffers from slow pacing, it succeeds as a cathartic sendoff for Nolan’s Batman.

A deeply flawed finale to Nolan’s groundbreaking take on the caped crusader. Christopher Nolan’s action thriller The Dark Knight Rises (2012) is a huge departure from the dark, gritty noir world of Batman Begins and The Dark Knight. Nolan goes for broke with ambitious action sequences with poor choreography for the hand to hand combat and ridiculous Batwing getting chased by missles aerial combat. I loved Batman and Bane’s sewer confrontation, but their final encounter is very basic. I finally find The Dark Knight Rises entertaining, but it is a letdown after Batman Begins and The Dark Knight for sure. Lee Smith’s editing is too disjointed as he cuts into dramatic moments with action or vice versa too often. This gets weary for a 165 minute run-time. Wally Pfister has some killer shots like Batman’s first appearance on the Batcycle or Bane’s fighting in the sewers. But much of his wide shots are duller this time around with no help from the blander New York backdrop instead of the striking Chicago skyline from the previous two installments. The production design is so cold and forgettable. Christian Bale is still magnificent as a broken and disheartened Bruce Wayne in all his brooding contemplation. His Batman is still fearsome, but they should have given Bale better choreography as it’s a step back from the first two films. Bale gives Bruce Wayne all his heart and care due to his serious method acting style. I appreciate how much Bale cared about this character. He did Batman justice throughout Nolan’s The Dark Knight Trilogy. I still love the Batsuit, Bane’s fur coat and mask, Catwoman’s ear goggles and skintight black suit, but Talia should have gotten more armor or something. In fact, Talia al Ghul is wasted completely as a predictable twist that isn’t shocking since Marion Cotillard is the only other woman besides Anne Hathaway’s sultry and playful Catwoman. I don’t even want to talk about how bad her death scene is with a poor take from the wonderful Cotillard just dropping her head suddenly in a comical way. I guess Christopher Nolan just missed that? On the other hand, I really like Hathaway’s take on Catwoman with her killer, effortless fighting style and femme fatale attitude, but she’s not Michelle Pfeiffer. Hathaway is gorgeous and smolders for the camera with an effortless charm and cool persona for her Selina Kyle. I actually wish Nolan focused more on Anne’s Catwoman as she has nice chemistry with Bale. Tom Hardy’s Bane is imposing due to his massive muscles and behemoth stature,but his choice of accent makes Bane sound like a drunken Sean Connery. He does kill certain shadowy lines and scary attacks, but his mouth being covered by the mask does obscure his voice enough that some lines are hard to hear. Others are booming loud and thrown right at you, which I appreciate the attempts at sound mixing for his masked vocals. I still think Bane’s character is too dumb to have plotted any of this movie, so it was also obvious Talia or some other shadow figure was the mastermind. I wish Nolan had gone with The Riddler or Harley Quinn as The Dark Knight Rises’ antagonist as Bane is too thin as a character to carry the film. Hardy is solid and intimidating enough during the necessary violent scenes. Michael Caine is emotional as Alfred Pennyworth, but he goes into melodramatic territory instead of heartbreaking delivery like the last two features. Gary Oldman sadly gets little to do as Commissioner James Gordon gets injured early on only to mentor Joseph Gordon-Levitt’s Robin into becoming a thoughtful and perceptive detective. I do wish we got a Robin or Nightwing film from Nolan with Levitt. I liked the cameos from Morgan Freeman, Matthew Modine, Ben Mendelsohn, Cillian Murphy, Liam Neeson, Juno Temple, Aidan Gillen, Tom Conti, Joey King, Thomas Lennon, and John Nolan. Tom Conti is particularly endearing and interesting as the helpful prisoner in Bane’s pit. Overall, the cast is apt for the challenge, but the ladies are not given enough lines or action compared to Batman or Bane. Catwoman and Talia al Ghul could have been so much more. Christopher Nolan and Jonathan Nolan’s writing is their career worst with silly, predictable twists and pathetic attempts at social commentary with their entire kill the rich plot. It’s dark and miserable, but I do not hate this movie anymore. I find much of Nolan’s direction riveting and exciting, especially alongside Hans Zimmer’s blaringly loud score. Bruce Wayne climbing from the pit is exciting and inspiring. Bane robbing the stock exchange is exciting as is Batman’s first appearance on the Batcycle to aid the hostages. The Dark Knight Rises might as well be Speed with Batman since it’s all action. Nolan’s enthralling direction elevates average material for a disappointing finale to his legendary Dark Knight Trilogy.

**The Dark Knight Rises – 3.5 Stars** A fitting, if flawed, finale to Nolan’s grounded Batman saga. *The Dark Knight Rises* is a pure action blockbuster with a stacked cast and some genuinely epic moments—Bane’s stadium speech still gives chills. Gotham looks more like dirty Manhattan than a stylized comic-book city, and the world feels oddly empty at times. But this entry finally dips deeper into comic lore, especially with Bane. His brutal takedown of Batman is straight out of *Knightfall*, and Bruce’s journey back feels like a mix of classic redemption arcs, even if the pit isn’t from *The Dark Knight Returns*. Hardy’s Bane may not look like the comic version, but he sells the threat completely—voice quirks aside. It doesn’t match the greatness of *The Dark Knight*, but I enjoyed this one more than *Batman Begins* thanks to its bigger comic-book feel. Still a shame they teased Robin with Joseph Gordon-Levitt... and then did nothing with it.

Revisiting TDKR, it’s rather clunky and overstuffed in a Spider-Man 3 (Raimi) kind of way. The staging of many scenes and plot points has that rushed, confusing and chaotic Nolan trademark that confuses ‘lots of things happening really quickly’ with style. One example, the fingerprint exchange scene with Cat woman and what’s his face took me at least two views to figure out what was happening exactly. After I did I had a sort of befuddled ‘oh ok’, there’s really nothing too clever here reaction. We don’t get a lot of Batman either, and the fight scenes are nothing to write home about. Choppy editing, and rushed. But overall it’s got a lot of neat elements, Hardy and Bale are always in top form and the film brings the trilogy story arc together in an interesting and overall satisfying way.
A perfect end to one of the greatest movie trilogy of all time.
Nearly just as good as the dark Knight. Tom hardy is great as bane. A great end to a great trilogy.

Thrilling, motivating, engaging, surprising, and extremely action packed, THE DARK KNIGHT RISES literally showcases the legacy of Batman at its finest, and is a true masterpiece in its own right. I’m really glad that this incredible trilogy ended on a high note.
